responses.strategic_rationale: The five levers focus on (1) data fidelity from residential, longitudinal capture of NREM parasomnias, (2) ethically sound and efficient recruitment/retention, (3) robust event-triage and annotation throughput, (4) privacy-driven governance and controlled external collaboration, and (5) scalable staffing and facility utilization. Balancing safety, data quality, cost, and scalability is critical given the plan’s long horizon and ambitious aims (phenotyping and semi-automated triage). The levers collectively address speed versus depth, openness versus privacy, within-site capacity versus cross-site replication, and resource constraints, while maintaining the plan’s core requirement for a physical Bonn location and serious data stewardship.

levers.name: Residential-NREM Parasomnia Data Capture Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiate nightly sensing across participants using dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors with time-synchronization to EEG-BIDS on NAS. Systemic: Data capture throughput increases by 25% per cohort, with usable data yield improving by 12% due to standardized timestamps and artifact handling, enabling a 10% faster scoring cycle. Strategic: Establish a scalable, privacy-preserving longitudinal data platform enabling robust phenotyping, cross-cohort replication, and longitudinal analyses while maintaining regulatory and safety constraints. Trade-offs: data richness vs privacy; throughput vs data depth; domestic naturalistic environment vs clinical control.
options: Maintain baseline low-burden sensing while enforcing tighter privacy controls and SOPs to preserve data quality.
Scale data capture by adding one additional active suite and standardizing nightly data pipelines to boost throughput.
Adopt a data-sharing-as-a-service platform with cloud-native ML-powered event triage and an external partner revenue-share model.
levers.review_lever: Controls data richness vs privacy risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory variability across EU privacy regimes and local housing safety constraints.

levers.name: Participant-Centric Recruitment and Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Implement enhanced pre-admission screening with structured collateral history collection and targeted pre-screening tests to optimize eligibility alignment. Systemic: Enrollment throughput rises by 20% over the first year, with screening-to-enrollment conversion improving from 12% to 18%, and participant burden metrics staying within acceptable limits. Strategic: Build a sustainable recruitment ecosystem that prioritizes safety, ethics, and high-quality data while enabling steady enrollment growth. Trade-offs: speed vs participant well-being/ethics; breadth of recruitment vs data consistency.
options: Strengthen structured pre-admission screening, optimize collateral history collection, and tighten eligibility criteria to safeguard safety.
Expand retention via flexible scheduling, enhanced participant engagement, and tiered compensation to improve stay completion.
Launch a participatory recruitment model with dynamic consent and community advisory input to broaden access while preserving privacy.
levers.review_lever: Controls recruitment throughput vs participant burden/ethics. Weakness: The options overlook long-term retention costs and potential biases from self-selected samples.

levers.name: Event-Triage and Annotation Pipeline Optimization Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Standardize scoring guidelines and implement double-scoring for initial data to solidify reliability. Systemic: Annotation throughput increases by 20% and inter-rater agreement improves from 0.75 to 0.82, enabling faster curation and reducing adjudication backlog by ~15%. Strategic: Build a scalable enrichment pipeline for phenotyping that can be extended to multi-site datasets and future automation while preserving human-in-the-loop validity. Trade-offs: throughput vs accuracy; automation vs interpretability.
options: Standardize scoring guidelines and implement double-scoring to improve reliability.
Automate artifact rejection and optimize event tagging with scheduled joint scorer reviews to increase throughput.
Integrate ML-powered event triage with federated learning across collaborating centers and a data-sharing-as-a-service platform for scalable external collaboration.
levers.review_lever: Controls throughput and scoring quality. Weakness: The options fail to address cross-site standardization and potential bias in scoring across centers.

levers.name: Privacy-First Data Sharing and Governance Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enforce strict de-identification and privacy-preserving storage with formal data-use agreements. Systemic: Time-to-access for external researchers increases by 40%, privacy risk is reduced by 60%, and compliance overhead rises by 8%. Strategic: Establish a governance framework enabling responsible external collaboration while upholding stringent privacy and ethical standards. Trade-offs: openness vs privacy; external collaboration vs control.
options: Enforce strict de-identification and privacy-preserving storage with formal data-use agreements.
Develop an audit-trail and consent-managed data access portal with tiered researcher permissions.
Adopt a dynamic consent framework with a data-sharing-as-a-service platform for controlled external use.
levers.review_lever: Controls privacy vs collaboration. Weakness: The options underplay governance interoperability across partner institutions.

levers.name: Scalable Hybrid PSG Load and Staffing Optimization Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Maintain current coverage with a conservative buffer, expanding only if data quality metrics remain within predefined thresholds. Systemic: Staffing utilization improves by 15% with a 10% gain in scheduling flexibility, and safety margins increase by 20%, enabling cautious scaling. Strategic: Position the unit to scale with new tech and a flexible workforce while preserving safety, data integrity, and participant experience. Trade-offs: cost control vs flexibility; safety vs scalability.
options: Maintain current coverage with conservative buffer and expand only if data quality metrics remain within thresholds.
Increase active suites by one, optimize staffing with data-driven shift schedules to improve safety margins and throughput.
Implement a telepresence-enabled on-demand staffing model and robotics-assisted triage with a data-sharing-as-a-service platform to scale operations.
levers.review_lever: Controls safety vs scalability. Weakness: The options do not fully address staff burnout risk and regulatory constraints for wearable deployment.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is cross-institutional replication and external ethics governance. Add 'Adopt a dynamic consent framework with a data-sharing-as-a-service platform for controlled external use' to Lever 4.

responses.strategic_rationale: The five new levers extend the plan’s focus from data capture and privacy governance to robust longitudinal analytics, adaptive recruitment, external validation, operational resilience, and participant welfare. Together they push toward scalable, reproducible science within a safe Bonn-based facility, while preserving ethical standards and participant trust. These levers balance data richness, study throughput, cross-site generalizability, and resource constraints, ensuring that the project remains feasible yet ambitious enough to yield transferable methods and credible findings suitable for phase-two funding and multi-center collaboration.

levers.name: Longitudinal-Phenotyping and Computational Modeling Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiate seed data pipelines across cohorts with standardized metadata and a versioned scoring schema. Systemic: Phenotype-feature stability improves by 15% and annotation throughput increases by 22%, with inter-rater reliability rising from 0.80 to 0.86. Strategic: Build a scalable, cross-site compatible modeling framework enabling transferable phenotypes and multi-site replication. Trade-offs: data richness vs privacy; modeling complexity vs interpretability.
options: Develop a structured phenotyping ontology and standardized metadata schema to improve reproducibility.
Integrate machine-learning driven pattern discovery with privacy-preserving data subsets to refine phenotypes.
Establish a federated analytics consortium with interoperable APIs and a governance framework for cross-site modeling.
levers.review_lever: Controls data richness vs privacy risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory variability across EU privacy regimes and local housing safety constraints.

levers.name: Adaptive Recruitment and Stage-Gated Enrollment Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establish staged enrollment with go/no-go decision points based on interim safety and data-quality metrics. Systemic: Enrollment cadence improves by 25% with safety incidents within tolerances; planning complexity rises by 12%. Strategic: Create a flexible recruitment path that accelerates progress while maintaining ethical safeguards. Trade-offs: speed vs safety; planning complexity vs operational agility.
options: Implement stage gates with predefined stop criteria to protect safety and data quality.
Introduce rolling enrollment with interim analyses to accelerate progress while maintaining ethical oversight.
Adopt a staged adaptive design reallocating resources to high-performing cohorts and expanding enrollment to meet signal.
levers.review_lever: Controls recruitment throughput vs participant burden/ethics. Weakness: The options overlook long-term retention costs and potential biases from self-selected samples.

levers.name: External Validation and Data-Ecosystem Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Pilot external validation with a de-identified dataset from a collaborating center. Systemic: Generalizability gains 20% as core phenotypes replicate across sites; external governance overhead increases by 12%. Strategic: Build credibility, reproducibility, and broader impact through an engaged data ecosystem. Trade-offs: openness vs control; data heterogeneity vs standardization.
options: Proactively establish data-use agreements and de-identification standards for external datasets to enable safe validation.
Create a shared, version-controlled analysis framework with reproducibility guidelines for external collaborators.
Launch a cross-institutional federation for real-time cross-site validation with joint governance and co-authored publications.
levers.review_lever: Controls cross-site generalizability vs governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to address cross-site standardization of scoring and calibration across centers.

levers.name: Resource-Constrained Operations Resilience Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Implement lean operations with cross-trained staff and shift-swapping to cover nights with minimal overtime. Systemic: Overtime costs drop 25%, nightly coverage gaps reduce by 20%, and staff retention improves by 15%. Strategic: Build a resilient, cost-controlled operation that can scale with modest expansion while maintaining safety. Trade-offs: cost control vs flexibility; safety vs scalability.
options: Cross-train staff across night shifts and implement shift swapping to minimize coverage gaps.
Adopt a modular staffing model with prepaid overtime budgets and flexible pools to respond to variability.
Develop robotics-assisted triage and remote monitoring to drastically reduce on-site staffing needs.
levers.review_lever: Controls safety vs scalability. Weakness: The options do not fully address staff burnout risk and regulatory constraints for wearable deployment.

levers.name: Ethical-Privacy-Participant Welfare Expansion Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strengthen welfare oversight with monthly safety audits and enhanced participant-facing support. Systemic: Adverse events decrease by 15%, participant satisfaction rises by 8%, and ethics-oversight overhead increases by 10%. Strategic: Deepens trust, safeguards wellbeing, and enables sustainable participant engagement. Trade-offs: ethics and welfare vs speed; oversight overhead vs throughput.
options: Institute independent welfare monitoring with monthly safety audits to protect participants.
Expand consent with tiered, ongoing choices and visible withdrawal options to enhance autonomy.
Create a welfare reserve fund and welfare liaison role enabling rapid response to concerns and incidents.
levers.review_lever: Controls ethics vs speed. Weakness: The options underplay governance interoperability across partner institutions.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is real-time, cross-device data quality governance and calibration consistency across sensors and nights. Add 'Longitudinal Real-Time Data Quality Monitoring and Cross-Device Calibration Standardization' to Lever 6.

responses.strategic_rationale: The five new levers extend focus into sensor integrity, participant experience equity, transparent longitudinal analytics, cross-institutional collaboration readiness, and operational resilience. Together they bolster data fidelity, ethical engagement, reproducibility, multi-center generalizability, and continuity of operations, ensuring the Bonn unit can generate robust methods validation and phenotyping results that are credible to funders and collaborators while safeguarding participant welfare and compliance. These levers balance conservative data governance with innovative collaboration models and resilient infrastructure, enabling scalable growth and transferable insights beyond a single-site study.

levers.name: Cross-Sensor Calibration Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiate baseline cross-sensor calibration across all monitoring devices and implement automated drift checks during each participant stay. Systemic: Calibration drift reduced by 40%, lowering false-positive event triggers by 12% and improving data usability by 15 percentage points. Strategic: Establish a scalable cross-site calibration framework enabling robust phenotyping, reproducibility, and future multi-center replication. Trade-offs: calibration accuracy vs privacy preservation; throughput vs calibration overhead.
options: Maintain baseline cross-sensor calibration with stringent validation to minimize drift while preserving privacy.
Institute a structured cross-site calibration protocol and periodic audits to improve sensor alignment comparability across cohorts.
Establish a federated analytics consortium with edge-native processing and secure enclaves for real-time cross-site validation while preserving privacy.
levers.review_lever: Controls calibration accuracy vs privacy preservation. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory variability across EU privacy regimes and local housing safety constraints.

levers.name: Participant Experience Equity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Enhance participant-facing communications, accessibility, and scheduling flexibility to lower entry barriers. Systemic: Stay completion rate rises by 18 percentage points and dropout rate falls by 25%, improving data representativeness. Strategic: Build a welfare-aligned participation framework that broadens access while maintaining safety and data quality. Trade-offs: participant-centric design increases administrative overhead and may introduce standardization frictions.
options: Introduce accessible communications, culturally sensitive materials, and flexible scheduling to broaden participation while preserving safety.
Increase retention through adaptive compensation and enhanced participant support to improve stay completion.
Establish a dynamic consent process with staged options and community engagement to broaden access while maintaining privacy.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant retention vs ethics and burden. Weakness: The options overlook long-term retention costs and potential biases from self-selected samples.

levers.name: Longitudinal-Analytics Transparency Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Publish a pre-registered analysis plan and maintain internal dashboards for governance. Systemic: Reproducibility index improves by 20%, with cross-site replication attempts increasing by 50%. Strategic: Build credibility and attract collaborations by elevating transparency, while managing data privacy concerns. Trade-offs: openness vs privacy; speed vs thoroughness.
options: Publish analysis plan and maintain internal dashboards to enhance transparency and oversight.
Institute a reproducibility audit with version-controlled protocols and blinded data handling to improve credibility.
Launch a federated analytics platform with on-site edge processing and open governance templates to enable cross-center collaboration.
levers.review_lever: Controls data transparency vs privacy leakage. Weakness: The options fail to address consistent calibration of scoring across centers.

levers.name: Cross-Institutional Collaboration Readiness Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Formalize MOUs and joint governance with partner centers. Systemic: Onboard two partner centers; cross-site validation success rate rises by 15 percentage points (to 85%). Strategic: Enable robust generalization and accelerate multi-center validation to support phase-two grant readiness. Trade-offs: governance burden vs collaboration reach; IP rights vs openness.
options: Formalize MOUs and common governance to align objectives and data-sharing expectations.
Create joint scoring templates and shared data access templates to ensure comparability.
Launch a cross-institutional collaboration co-op powered by a cloud-native research hub with federated authentication and tiered data access to enable rapid multi-center validation.
levers.review_lever: Controls governance burden vs collaboration reach. Weakness: The options do not fully address IP ownership and publication rights across consortium members.

levers.name: Operational Risk Management and Redundancy Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Implement redundancy across critical data streams and conduct quarterly incident drills. Systemic: Downtime reduced by 40%, data-loss risk decreased by 75%, strengthening study continuity. Strategic: Create a resilient, scalable operations posture enabling sustained studies and external collaborations. Trade-offs: resilience vs cost efficiency; complexity vs agility.
options: Implement redundancy across critical data streams and run quarterly incident drills to reduce outages.
Proactively hedge with modular on-demand staffing and scalable backup infrastructure to maintain continuity.
Pilot a cloud-orchestrated disaster-recovery suite with autonomous failover and remote data replication to minimize downtime during incidents.
levers.review_lever: Controls resilience vs cost efficiency. Weakness: The options do not fully address regulatory constraints for remote operations and data sovereignty.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is cross-institutional governance readiness and real-time cross-device data quality monitoring. Add 'Real-time data quality dashboard with cross-device drift alerts' to Lever 11.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts that are specific to this project's domain (e.g., "[Domain]-[Decision Type] Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
